title: The asthmatic whose nose ran and airway closed after one aspirin
blurb: Nasal polyps, asthma, and a near-fatal reaction to a common painkiller. Blocking one branch of a lipid pathway forces everything down the other.

patient.complaint: Acute wheeze, nasal congestion, and facial flushing 45 minutes after taking aspirin for a headache.

opening: A 34-year-old woman with adult-onset asthma and recurrent nasal polyps develops rhinorrhea, periorbital flushing, and severe bronchospasm ~45 minutes after taking aspirin. She has had milder versions of this after ibuprofen. She improves with bronchodilators and reports her asthma has always been 'the hard-to-control kind.' What do you want to ask, examine, or order next?
history: Triad of asthma, chronic rhinosinusitis with nasal polyps, and reactions to aspirin/NSAIDs. No reaction to acetaminophen or COX-2-selective agents. No true IgE-mediated urticaria. Symptoms reproducible with any nonselective COX inhibitor.
exam: Diffuse expiratory wheeze, prolonged expiration. Nasal exam: pale, boggy mucosa with visible polyps. Periorbital and facial flushing. No urticaria or angioedema of the classic allergic type. O₂ sat 92% during the episode.

prompts.cause: Which enzyme does aspirin block, and what happens to the substrate as a result?
prompts.mechanism: Why does blocking that enzyme paradoxically make the airway WORSE?

accepts: loss of pge2 brake plus leukotriene overproduction

pinpoint.prompt: Click the enzyme step aspirin blocks — the block that shunts substrate the other way.
pinpoint.correctMsg: Yes — cyclooxygenase (Arachidonate → PGH₂). Aspirin/NSAIDs block COX, so (1) the bronchoprotective prostaglandin PGE₂ is lost and (2) arachidonate is shunted down 5-lipoxygenase to cysteinyl leukotrienes (LTC₄/D₄/E₄) — potent bronchoconstrictors and mucus secretagogues. In AERD these patients already overproduce leukotrienes, so removing the COX 'brake' triggers a severe reaction. Note acetaminophen and COX-2-selective drugs are usually tolerated.
pinpoint.wrongMsg: Not quite — aspirin acetylates the enzyme that makes PGH₂ (prostaglandins/thromboxane). Blocking it pushes arachidonate toward leukotrienes.
pinpoint.revealMsg: The answer was COX (Arachidonate → PGH₂): aspirin's target; blocking it shunts arachidonate to leukotrienes.

mcqs.stem: Which class of drug most directly addresses the mediator responsible for the bronchospasm here?
choices: Leukotriene-pathway drugs — montelukast (LTD₄-receptor antagonist) or zileuton (5-LOX inhibitor)
mcqs.expl: The cysteinyl leukotrienes drive the airway response, so blocking their receptor (montelukast) or their synthesis (zileuton, a 5-LOX inhibitor) is rational. Aspirin desensitization is also used for the polyps/sinus disease.

mcqs.stem: Why can these patients usually take acetaminophen and celecoxib safely?
choices: Acetaminophen is a weak COX inhibitor (especially peripherally) and celecoxib is COX-2-selective; the reaction is driven by strong COX-1 inhibition
mcqs.expl: AERD is a pharmacologic (not IgE-allergic) reaction to COX-1 inhibition. Agents that spare COX-1 — acetaminophen at usual doses, COX-2-selective celecoxib — generally don't provoke it.

mcqs.stem: How does low-dose aspirin's antiplatelet effect illustrate the same enzyme, differently?
choices: It has nothing to do with COX
Aspirin irreversibly acetylates platelet COX-1 → no thromboxane A₂; platelets can't resynthesize the enzyme, so the effect lasts the platelet's lifespan (~7-10 days)
mcqs.expl: Same enzyme (COX), same drug — but here the therapeutic target is platelet thromboxane. Because platelets are anucleate and can't make new COX, a single irreversible acetylation disables them for their whole lifespan, which is why low-dose aspirin is dosed daily.
